Predicting the mass of prehistoric flying animals

In order to predict the weight of prehistoric birds, we will use wingspan and mass data from modern bats to create linear models

library(devtools)
Loading required package: usethis
library(rlang)

And then install the Bat Wing git hub package using:

pak::pak("oleon12/BatWing")
 
ℹ No downloads are needed
✔ 1 pkg: kept 1 [3.3s]

We can load and preview the bat wing data using:

library(BatWing)

data(wing)
head(wing)
         id ForearmLength Digit5.Length Digit4.Length Digit3.Length Mass
1 ODLAM-011          41.4         63.20         61.70         87.50 15.5
2 ODLAM-010          42.4         64.10         62.60         94.90 17.5
3 ODLAM-012          37.9         54.60         58.20         76.00  9.0
4 ODLAM-014          41.5         58.30         58.95         79.70 16.5
5 ODLAM-016          31.0         28.40         49.90         60.75  7.5
6 ODLAM-018          42.1         59.15         61.90         79.90 15.5
  Wingspan
1      330
2      325
3      295
4      319
5      238
6      331

Finding LSA (lifting surface area)


Use this command to calculate the LSA according to the “BM” and “SS” formulas:

“BM” = Blood and McFarlane 1988
“SS” = Smith and Starett 1979

“BM”:

LSA = 2* [(FA*V) +1/2(V * III)]

“SS”:

LSA = 2[(FA*V) +1/2*(cos(a)III*IV) +1/2*(sin(a)IV * V)]

{ found in https://github.com/oleon12/BatWing }

LSA <- BatWingArea(x=wing, scale=TRUE, method=c("BM","SS"))
head(LSA$Area)
                   BM          SS
ODLAM-011 0.010762960 0.011892700
ODLAM-010 0.011518770 0.012604629
ODLAM-012 0.008288280 0.009585018
ODLAM-014 0.009485410 0.010660039
ODLAM-016 0.003486100 0.005107123
ODLAM-018 0.009706515 0.011134029
print(head(LSA$Area))
                   BM          SS
ODLAM-011 0.010762960 0.011892700
ODLAM-010 0.011518770 0.012604629
ODLAM-012 0.008288280 0.009585018
ODLAM-014 0.009485410 0.010660039
ODLAM-016 0.003486100 0.005107123
ODLAM-018 0.009706515 0.011134029

{The scale=TRUE function turns the measurements from millimeters and grams to meters and kilograms}

I will merge the Area from the LSA data frame and the mass from the wing dataframe in order to simplify the creation of linear models.

df <- data.frame(
    Area = LSA$Area,   # or whatever your column is actually called
    Mass = wing$Mass/1000
)

dividing mass by 1000 converts the units of grams into units of kilograms.

Creating linear models

I will now create 3 linear models.


The first lm (model1) is created using Mass as a function of the Area as calculated by the Blood and McFarlane formula (1988)

The second lm (model2) is created using Mass as a function of the Area as calculated by the Smith and Starret formula (1979)

The third lm (model3) is created using Mass as a function of the Wingspan

Mass ~ Area.BM

model1 <- lm(Mass ~ Area.BM, data = df)
summary(model1)

Call:
lm(formula = Mass ~ Area.BM, data = df)

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-0.006051 -0.002632 -0.001088  0.003460  0.007376 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 0.002144   0.003390   0.632  0.54482    
Area.BM     1.557333   0.306418   5.082  0.00095 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.004546 on 8 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7635,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.734 
F-statistic: 25.83 on 1 and 8 DF,  p-value: 0.0009503
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = Area.BM, y = Mass)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Mass ~ Area.SS

model2 <- lm(Mass ~ Area.SS, data = df)
summary(model2)

Call:
lm(formula = Mass ~ Area.SS, data = df)

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-0.005759 -0.002220 -0.001220  0.003477  0.006952 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 0.001413   0.003399   0.416 0.688626    
Area.SS     1.392434   0.264068   5.273 0.000753 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.004419 on 8 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7766,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.7486 
F-statistic:  27.8 on 1 and 8 DF,  p-value: 0.0007525
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(df, aes(x = Area.SS, y = Mass)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Mass ~ Wingspan

For model 3, we need to make a new columns on the wing df which contains the mass and wingspan in units of m and kg, that way we can better comprehend the data.

wing$Mass_kg <- wing$Mass / 1000
wing$Wingspan_m <- wing$Wingspan / 1000

model3 <- lm(Mass_kg ~ Wingspan_m, data = wing)
summary(model3)

Call:
lm(formula = Mass_kg ~ Wingspan_m, data = wing)

Residuals:
       Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
-0.0065853 -0.0015683  0.0003694  0.0027778  0.0047156 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -0.017354   0.005765   -3.01 0.016815 *  
Wingspan_m   0.104072   0.016704    6.23 0.000251 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.003864 on 8 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8291,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8078 
F-statistic: 38.82 on 1 and 8 DF,  p-value: 0.000251
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(wing, aes(x = Wingspan_m, y = Mass_kg)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)
`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Analysis of linear models:

Model 3, the one that Predicts Mass from Wingspan has the highest Adjusted R-squared value, which leads me to believe that this model will yield the highest quality predictions. This is beneficial because it is also the simplest model.

Prediction of mass based off of wingspan. We will be predicting the mass of the Quetzacoatlus and Pterodactylus dinosaurs

According to https://www.britannica.com/animal/pterodactyl ,

The wingspan of the Quetzacoatlus may have been 2 - 11 meters! this is a very large range, which is not ideal for mass prediction, however, we can determine a preliminary range

The wingspan of the Pterodactylus may have been 0.50 to 1 meter


These mass predictions will not account for factors such as:

prehistoric air density, size of the head and neck, wingbeat frequency (how many times a wing is pumped during an interval of time), ect.

However, the wingspan data should give us a rough range of mass values, a fine starting point in this analysis.

We will create new dataframes which contain the wingspan length according to brittanica:

Creating predictions for quetzacoatlus:

quetz <- data.frame(Wingspan_m = c(2,11))
predictions_q <- predict(model3, newdata = quetz)
print(predictions_q)
       1        2 
0.190791 1.127441

Creating predictions for pterodactylus:

ptero <- data.frame(Wingspan_m = c(0.50,1))
predictions_p <- predict(model3, newdata = ptero)
print(predictions_p)
         1          2 
0.03468256 0.08671869

This data suggests that the quetzacoatlus may have weighed between 0.191 - 1.127 kg

This suggests that the pterodactylus may have weighed between 0.034 - 0.087 kg!

This number does not make any sense. The quetzacoatlus depicted in the picture above is much large than 1.127 kg.

What have we learned:


The relationship between wingspan and mass of a winged creature is not linear


Determining the mass of a winged creature is much more complicated than anticipated.


If we try to use the known wingspan of a bee and compare the known weight of a bee to the predicted weight of a bee, we will find that the predicted weight is in the negative.

bee <- data.frame(Wingspan_m = c(0.018,0.020))
predictions_b <- predict(model3, newdata = bee)
print(predictions_b)
          1           2 
-0.01548027 -0.01527213

What this indicates is that our linear model is too inaccurate for all sizes of winged creature.

This means that the model is very specific to bats.

Conclusion:

The weight of a winged creature is not linearly dependent on the wingspan of said creature.

These conclusions are not very satisfying, however, this does tell us that the physics determining the lift of a creature is much more complex than the length of the wings.
